coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots in fluorographene matrix formed by means of chemical functionalization
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nebogatikova, N.A.
Antonova, I.V.
Prinz, V. Ya.
Timofeev, V.B.
Smagulova, S.A.
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, we examined the creation conditions and the properties of graphene or multi-layer graphene (MLG) quantum dots (QDs) self-formed in insulating dielectric fluorographene matrix. The QD arrays were obtained using chemical functionalization of graphene or MLG films in an aqueous solution of hydrofluoric acid. The formation of a fluorographene network in the films caused their transition from conducting to insulating state; this transition was a result of partitioning of the films into isolated graphene islands accompanied by an increase of the resistance of the films by 7–8 orders of magnitude. Experimentally, it was shown that the characteristic time required for the formation of QD arrays in the films depended linearly on the initial film thickness, on the solution concentration, and on temperature. The atomic force microscopy lateral force measurements have allowed us to evaluate the sizes and densities of formed QDs, and to trace the evolution of both QDs and fluorinated barriers between them in the functionalization process. A possibility to achieve QD sizes ranging from 70–20nm (at QD density ∼(4–6)×1010 cm−2) to 10–20nm (at QD density ∼1×1010 cm−2) has been demonstrated. The activation energies of electrical conductivity in the films with QDs have been evaluated.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of samples and experimental procedure
Conditions for the formation of graphene quantum dot arrays in fluorographene matrix
The field effect and hysteresis in the current–voltage characteristics
The structure of layers with QDs
Electrical properties of graphene QDs and fluorographene insulating barriers in partially fluorinated films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Threshold treatment duration required for reaching the conductor–insulator transition t th versus the film thickness for different compositions of the aqueous HF solutions or their vapors. By way of example, the inset shows film resistivity values measured on applying voltages ±5V versus the duration of treatment of the samples in the 2.5-% solution of HF in water. Sample thickness: 1 – one monolayer, 2–5nm. (b) Time t th versus the solution concentration for 5-nm thick samples at room temperature.

(a): Source-drain current–voltage characteristics versus the voltage applied between planar contacts for a sample whose degree of fluorination was at the transition to insulator. Data for various temperatures are shown. (b) Transistor characteristics of the same sample, or the curves of source-drain current versus gate voltage, measured at room temperature with the silicon substrate used as the gate. Parts (с) and (d) of the figure shows the sketches of electric circuits that were used to perform measurements (a) and (b), respectively. In the cases (b) and (d), the drain-source voltage V ds was 0.2V. For all measurements, the rate of voltage sweep was 0.5V/s.

(a) SEM image of the surface of a 4-nm thick MLG film treated in a solution of HF in water. In the image, large light folds that have formed during the transfer of films onto the silicon dioxide substrate are clearly seen. (b) Enlarged fragment of image (a). Functionalization of film regions in the vicinity of domain boundaries and buckling of fluorinated regions lead to the formation of buckled regions that look in the image as light zones stretching along domain boundaries. (c) Schematic representation of a domain boundary prior to and after a fluorination procedure. In the non-fluorinated film, the film surface was a smooth surface exhibiting no corrugations.

(a) Image of the surface of a 7-nm thick MLG film treated in a solution of HF in water during 5min, i.e. for a treatment duration in the region of the transition to insulating state (fluorination time roughly about t th). (b) Enlarged fragment of image (a).

Surface morphology of a MLG film versus the duration of functionalization of the film in a 2.5-% solution of HF in water. (a) image of the surface of a 1.5-nm thick film treated in the solution during 4min (t ∼ t th); the image was obtained in surface-relief scan mode. (b) Image of the same surface simultaneously registered in lateral force mode. (c) and (d) Image of the surface of the same sample registered in AFM lateral force measurements after 10- and 20-min functionalization treatments.

(a) Sizes of graphene islands in insulating fluorographene matrix versus the duration of treatment of samples in a solution of HF in water. (b) The density of graphene islands in insulating fluorographene matrix per unit surface area of samples versus HF treatment duration. The insets explain which experimental data were used in evaluating the sizes and concentrations of QDs.

The saturation current at positive bias voltages (see Fig. 2a) versus reciprocal temperature for a 1.5-nm thick film whose degree of fluorination corresponded to the occurrence of a conductor–insulator transition in the film. The various symbols refer to measurements that were performed repeatedly on one and the same structure. E1 and E2 are the activation energies extracted from the linear parts of I(103/T) curves. The inset shows the supposed band diagram of the film with graphene QDs in fluorographene matrix.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots in fluorographene matrix formed by means of chemical functionalization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rzhanov Institute of Semiconductor Physics, SB RAS, Acad. Lavrent’ev Avenue 13, 630090 Novosibirsk, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Novosibirsk State University, Pirogova Street 2, 630090 Novosibirsk, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ammosov North-East Federal University, Belinskii Street 58, 677000 Yakutsk, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, we examined the creation conditions and the properties of graphene or multi-layer graphene (MLG) quantum dots (QDs) self-formed in insulating dielectric fluorographene matrix. The QD arrays were obtained using chemical functionalization of graphene or MLG films in an aqueous solution of hydrofluoric acid. The formation of a fluorographene network in the films caused their transition from conducting to insulating state; this transition was a result of partitioning of the films into isolated graphene islands accompanied by an increase of the resistance of the films by 7–8 orders of magnitude. Experimentally, it was shown that the characteristic time required for the formation of QD arrays in the films depended linearly on the initial film thickness, on the solution concentration, and on temperature. The atomic force microscopy lateral force measurements have allowed us to evaluate the sizes and densities of formed QDs, and to trace the evolution of both QDs and fluorinated barriers between them in the functionalization process. A possibility to achieve QD sizes ranging from 70–20nm (at QD density ∼(4–6)×1010 cm−2) to 10–20nm (at QD density ∼1×1010 cm−2) has been demonstrated. The activation energies of electrical conductivity in the films with QDs have been evaluated.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a material one atom thick with a high mobility of charge carriers and a high thermal conductivity; from this material, many graphene-based dielectrics such as oxidized, hydrogenated, and fluorinated graphene can be obtained [1–4]. Fabrication and properties of new graphene based materials involving quantum structures obtained by means of graphene local functionalization is of interest for various applications. QD systems offer materials having potential for many applications such as optoelectronic and memory devices, sensors, bio-imaging systems, medicine, etc. [5,6]. Arrays of QDs embedded in a non-conducting matrix are interesting objects for the study of energy-quantization induced quantum-dimensional effects, interaction between QDs, and charge transport in QD arrays [2,3]. Few(multi)-layered graphene with thickness up to few nanometers shares all the attractive properties demonstrated by graphene except for its linear dispersion spectrum and, simultaneously, it provides additional possibilities in fabrication of multi-layered graphene-based heterostructures [7]. The possibility to controllably modify the properties of graphene and MLG films while creating the derivatives of those materials with varied degree of functionalization and locality has stimulated interest in studying such structures as QDs (in graphene and MLG, QDs are regions with characteristic sizes less than 100–200nm [5,8,9]), nanoribbons, etc. Such studies were performed both for single structures and for arrays of isolated QDs.

The approach most frequently used for obtaining graphene-based QDs is the creation of QDs by nanolithography. In various experiments, the lateral sizes of QDs formed by this method ranged from 50 to 250nm [8,9]. While studying the conductivity in transistor structures with one QD at low (helium) temperatures, workers were also able to investigate various quantum-dimensional effects. Nonetheless, the latter approach is not meant for obtaining QD arrays and for examining the interaction between QDs.

Another method for obtaining isolated QDs is the chemical or ultrasonic disintegration of graphite in nanoflakes and preparation of graphene suspensions [10]. A specific feature of graphene flakes obtained by this method is the decoration of flake edges with various functional groups. With functional groups of several types involved, the stoichiometric composition may show variations depending on particular synthesis conditions of QDs. For instance, synthesis of oxifluorinated graphene has been demonstrated in [11].

The influence of functional groups on the properties of graphene QDs was discussed in [12], where QDs were obtained with the help of nanolithography and block copolymers used as a mask. In their study, the authors of [12] have obtained and examined arrays of QDs having identical sizes of 10 or 20nm, and they found no correlation between the spectral position of peaks in the photoluminescence spectra of their samples and the QD sizes. It was established that the oxidation of lattice defects with oxygen was a determining factor for the emergence of photoluminescence in examined structures. The functional groups forming and surrounding QDs can therefore be regarded as an important factor defining the properties of QDs.

In the present study, we have demonstrated an optimal condition to create arrays of graphene and MLG quantum dots self-formed during chemical functionalization of graphene in an aqueous solution or vapor of hydrofluoric acid, and results of their property investigation. This fluorination process was introduced by the present authors in [13,14]. The simplicity of the functionalization procedure, involvement of no corrosive media and high temperatures, and also the possibility of achieving controllable variation of QD sizes were advantageous features of the used method for obtaining QD arrays. As a result of our experiments, graphene or MLG conducting islands, or fluorographene-embedded graphene or MLG QDs, 70–10nm in diameter, available in our samples in densities ranging from 1×109 to 6×1010 cm−2 were obtained. In addition, the high stability of fluorographene matrix ensures, first, a high stability of obtained films with QD arrays and, second, stable functionalization of QD edges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples and experimental procedure
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene or MLG samples examined in the present study were obtained either by electrostatic exfoliation of highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) on oxidized silicon substrates with oxide thickness 300nm [15] or by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) of graphene onto copper substrates followed by transfer of deposited graphene sheets onto SiO2/Si substrates. It should be noted here that in both cases the obtained graphene and MLG films were made up by polycrystals with characteristic domain sizes ranging from 0.5 to 3μm. In the present study, graphene, bigraphene, and MLG films with thicknesses up to 6nm (∼20 atomic layers) were examined.

Functionalization of graphene and MLG films was achieved using two methods, either by treating samples in a solution of hydrofluoric acid (HF) in water or by exposing the samples to the vapor of the same solution. After treatment in HF, the samples were rinsed with deionized water during 5–10min. The highest fluorination rate was attained in solutions that contained 1.5–2.5% of HF. The former treatments typically lasted for several minutes.

For characterization of our samples, the following experimental techniques were used: optical microscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Raman spectroscopy (RS), and measurements of current–voltage characteristics.

A Solver PRO NT-MDT scanning microscope was employed for taking AFM images from the samples and substrates, and also for determining the thickness of our samples. AFM measurements were carried out both in contact and contactless modes. Apart from the study of surface relief, AFM lateral force measurements of friction forces were performed to visualize regions having different chemical compositions. Current–voltage characteristics of samples were measured using a Keithley picoampermeter (model 6485); for performing such measurements, two contacts prepared from silver alloy were applied onto the surface of each sample, and then a 20-min anneal was given to the sample at temperature 125°С to make the polymer component removed from the alloy. SEM images were taken using a JEOLJSM-7800F scanning electron microscope; the energy of primary electrons in this microscope was 2keV.
Due to simplicity of the developed fluorination procedure, many (a few hundred) samples were given fluorination treatments in our experiments. The results proved to be highly reproducible and stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions for the formation of graphene quantum dot arrays in fluorographene matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Consider first the main proofs showing that graphene and MLG films indeed undergo fluorination in aqueous solutions of HF [13,14]. First of all, fluorination is supported by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectroscopy (RS) data. In samples treated with a solution of HF in water, XPS spectra exhibit peaks due to bound fluorine ions (687.7eV) and carbon–fluorine chemical bonds (288eV) as it had to be in fluorinated samples [16]. In the Raman spectra of functionalized films, a decrease in the intensity of the G (∼1580cm−1) and 2D (∼2700cm−1) peaks detected in as-obtained films was observed, and those peaks vanished (reached the noise level) at sufficiently long treatment durations. Such transformations in the Raman spectra were due to the opening of bandgap in fluorographene (∼3.0–3.5eV) [16,17] and also due to the fact that the energy of laser-emitted photons (2.41eV) was insufficient for band-to-band excitation of the material causing the output of the resonance conditions. Such transformations in Raman spectra well correlate with available data for fluorinated graphene and MLG films [16,17]. A gradual reduction in the amplitude of the Raman peaks points to the fact that the degree of fluorination of the films gradually increased with treatment duration.

It was shown earlier (see [13,14]) that fluorination of graphene and MLG films is a thermally activated process (E act ∼1.4eV) that can be made proceeding in opposite direction by increasing the treatment temperature to ∼450°С (the activation energy of defluorination process is 2.0eV). The characteristics of supposed defluorination process coincide with available data for fluorographene [16]. It is worth noting that during treatment of samples in an aqueous HF solution two processes proceed simultaneously, one process being the functionalization of the films and the second process, the etching of silicon dioxide, including its etching under the graphene film. So, the fluorination of a film proved to be a bilateral process.

For further discussion of obtained data, we introduce the threshold time of sample treatment in HF, t th. Time t th is the time of an HF treatment of graphene or MLG films that leads to an abrupt change of many properties of the samples, in particular, to a transition of the films from conducting into insulating state. On the latter transition, the resistance increases by seven or eight orders of magnitude, reaching a value of 1012 Ohm/cm2 (see the inset in Fig. 1 a). Measured resistance data show a good correlation with available data for fluorographene obtained from graphene sheets by a traditional method, treatment of graphene sheets in XeF2 or in other fluorine-containing gases at elevated temperatures [16,17]. The abrupt growth of resistance is due to the formation of a connected network of fluorinated regions. In samples with such a network, the Raman spectra still exhibit G and 2D peaks whose intensity amounts to ∼0.1–0.07 of the initial intensity of the peaks. The latter observation implies that the film still contains isolated non-fluorinated regions. The dependences of the time t th on the film thickness and on the solution concentration are shown in Fig. 1а and b. Evidently, for thick films a longer time is required for such films to be transferred into isolating state. For thin films (<10nm), the time t th almost linearly depends on the film thickness; this dependence presumably results from successive (layer-by-layer) fluorination of the films. Also, it is seen that the shortest times required for reaching the resistance jump in the films were attained in a 2.5-% solution of HF in water. It should be noted here that an increase of the thickness of MLG films in excess of 10nm resulted either in a pronounced deceleration or complete termination of the fluorination process.

Based on the above data, we were able to choose optimal conditions for fluorination of graphene or MLG films in our samples. The optimal conditions proved to be as follows: room or elevated solution temperature and 1.5–2.5-% content of HF in the aqueous solution. Among the data shown in Fig. 1a, one point refers to MLG films that were treated with the vapor of 2.5-% solution of HF in water. The use of the vapor extends the capabilities of the method to the cases in which liquid treatments may appear undesirable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The field effect and hysteresis in the current–voltage characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Here, the electrical characteristics of the films with QDs formed by means of the HF treatment for the time ∼t th. The transition to insulating state was observed when a connected network of fluorinated domains, making graphene regions insulated from each other, formed in the films. The fluorinated regions acted as potential barriers for the current. As it will be shown below, the width of the barriers was minimal for the HF treatment duration at which the transition to insulating state occurred, and this width increased in time during further fluorination of our samples.

In films with the minimal width of fluorographene barriers, measurements of current–voltage characteristics under application of high DS bias voltages were performed with the aim to organize conditions for initiating a high electric conductivity achieved through a film with potential barriers in between graphene islands (voltage – up to 13V, separation between the planar contacts – 80μm). As a result, we have found that, indeed, an emergence of conductivity in structure with potential barriers occurred in the films at voltages over 7–8V (Fig. 2 a). On a decrease of the temperature at which the measurements were performed in the range from 350 down to 80K, no change in the voltage required for restoration of conductivity was observed; yet, the density of the current through film showed a distinct decrease. During the reverse sweep of voltage, a pronounced hysteresis (3–4V) was observed, this hysteresis being presumably due to the charge trapped either at graphene islands or at states in fluorographene barriers.

A pronounced field effect and hysteresis were also found to exist while performing measurements in transistor configuration, with the silicon substrate being used as the gate (Fig. 2b). The measuring circuit used in the latter case is shown in Fig. 2d. Here, the neutrality point in the current–voltage characteristic, or in the curve of source-drain current versus silicon-substrate potential, was found to exhibit a shift towards positive voltages, this direction of the shift being indicative of р-type conductivity of graphene/MLG with QDs (sweeping towards positive voltages). In sweeping the voltage in opposite direction, the current–voltage characteristic turned out shifted towards zero voltage, such a shift pointing to a reduced doping level.

For films with a higher fluorination degree, featuring, as it will be shown below, a greater width of fluorographene barriers in between QDs, biasing with voltages up to 15V no longer initiated conductivity.

Thus, measurements of current–voltage characteristics performed on samples after the transition to insulating state confirm the presence of conducting graphene islands in fluorographene matrix and demonstrate the trapping of charges in such films. Recall that the amplitude of the main Raman peaks in such samples amounted to approximately 0.1 of the initial amplitude, this finding also being indicative of only partial fluorination of the films. In addition, upon the transition to isolating state, the films exhibited a distinct change of their surface morphology; this change consisted in the formation of corrugations on the film surface. The latter phenomenon will be discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure of layers with QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Consider now structural transformations proceeding in the films during fluorination. Since our films were polycrystalline films, it is the defects and domain boundaries that were fluorinated first in the films. At this stage, the formation of fluorinated regions in our films was studied using scanning electron microscopy. An SEM image of a partially fluorinated sample (i.e. prior to the occurrence of the transition into insulating state) is shown in Fig. 3 a and b. As a result of fluorination, domain boundaries (a network of dark lines) have become distinctly observed. It is significant that in the vicinity of domain boundaries there appear buckled regions looking in the image as light regions stretching along domain boundaries. A schematic representation of fluorinated domain boundaries is shown in Fig. 3c. The film relief emerges as a result of non-uniform (local) strains arising during partial fluorination of the films (the lattice constant of fluorographene is approximately 1% greater than that of graphene [16,17]); as a result, buckling of fluorinated regions has occurred.

It is a well-known fact that domain boundaries are regions rich in lattice defects such as 5- and 7-membered rings of carbon (Fig. 3c) [18,19]. Such regions normally show a greater chemical reactivity in comparison with inner domain regions. On the other hand, vacancy defects present at domain boundaries are capable of providing access for the solution to underlying film layers, especially in the case of buckled upper layer. The observed transition into insulating state proves that fluorination has occurred throughout the whole film thickness, i.e. in all layers of the film. Yet, because of the difficult access of fluorinating complexes to inner film layers, we can expect that, on increasing the film thickness, a longer time will be required for higher fluorination of the film. In addition, a lower degree of fluorination in inner monolayers can be expected. As a result, the characteristic size of QDs, and also the degree of fluorination of the barriers isolating QDs may appear different in different film layers.

An AFM image of the surface of a film that was given an HF treatment sufficiently long for reaching the occurrence of the transition into insulating state (fluorination time of about t th) is shown in Fig. 4 a and b. Before exposure of the surface region shown in Fig. 4a and b to the aqueous solution of HF, this region had an atomically smooth surface. As it was noted above, the fluorination process in the film material first begins at domain boundaries, and then it penetrates into defectless regions. The latter regions are less reactive and more stable to the action of hydrofluoric acid. Yet, under the action of mechanical stresses, strains, and redistribution of electron density in neighboring fluorinated regions, chemical functionalization processes subsequently get initiated in defectless regions, too [20]. The characteristic sizes of formed MLG islands as it follows from Fig. 4a and b are 200–400nm, their density being ∼109 cm−2.

For studying the transformations that proceeded in graphene and MLG films during fluorination, AFM measurements were carried out not only in surface-relief scan mode, but also in lateral force mode. The latter measurements have allowed us to visualize fluorinated material parts on the film surface and make a comparison between images taken from the surface of samples with different degree of fluorination. Measurement of the lateral force acting between the probe and the sample proved to be a useful means for revealing the structure of partially fluorinated graphene films.

As it was noted above, the conductor–insulator transition was accompanied with the formation of corrugations on the film surface. AFM images taken during one and the same passes of the scanner over the surface of an MLG sample treated in the solution of hydrofluoric acid in water during a time t ∼ t th are shown in Fig. 5 a and b. It is seen that, as a result of the treatment, a relief of ∼100-nm period and 3–5nm height has formed on the surface of the sample. Performing lateral-force measurements, we have obtained an image of a «network» made up by fluorinated regions (Fig. 5b). The fluorinated regions in the figure have a lighter tint. The lighter is a particular region, the higher are both the friction the AFM probe “feels” as it moves over the surface and the degree of fluorination of the surface area under study. The dark areas are graphene regions which still remained non-fluorinated. The density of such regions on unit surface was (4–6)×1010 cm−2, and their sizes were 20–70nm. The fact that the dark regions were indeed graphene regions is confirmed by reference measurements performed on initial non-fluorinated films.

Simultaneous registration of one and the same surface region in various scan modes has allowed us to correlate the various surface features observed in different taken images. It is seen that points at the boundaries of the fluorinated network in Fig. 5b refer to the regions with the highest buckling height in Fig. 5a. Graphene islands (darker surface regions in Fig. 5b) are localized at the «bottom» of the corrugated surface shown in Fig. 5a. It is precisely such a structure that had to be expected on the basis of the mechanism underlying the formation of the films, – buckling of fluorinated regions accompanied with the relaxation of generated stresses.

From the above mechanism of gradual fluorination of graphene and MLG films in aqueous HF solution, it follows that, with increasing the treatment duration both the sizes and the total number of graphene islands per unit surface area should show a decrease. Data confirming the latter prediction are shown in Fig. 5с and d, and in Fig. 6 а and b. In the image of Fig. 5b (4-min treatment of the sample in 2.5-% solution of HF in water), the size of graphene islands can be estimated as 20–70nm, and their density, as (4–6)×1010 cm−2. After 10-min treatment, the size of graphene islands has decreased to 10–40nm, and their density, to ∼1.4×1010 cm−2 (Fig. 5с). The curves of the sizes and density of graphene islands versus treatment duration are shown in Fig.6. The decrease in the number of graphene islands per unit surface area observed with prolonging the duration of HF treatment can be attributed to the fact that, by that time, part of graphene islands had already undergone more complete fluorination.

To summarize, as a result of fluorination, arrays of QDs formed in our samples, and the sizes and density of the QDs showed a decrease on prolonging the duration of HF treatment. In the latter situation, the separation between QDs, or the width of fluorinated regions, increases, and we have a possibility to directionally vary the QD sizes and the width of fluorographene barriers in between QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical properties of graphene QDs and fluorographene insulating barriers in partially fluorinated films
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene QDs obtained via fluorination of graphene or MLG films form as contained in non-conducting fluorographene matrix. As it was shown above, in samples with relatively thin fluorinated inter-QD barriers, an enhanced conductivity could be initiated by applying a high bias voltage to the sample (see Fig. 2). On sweeping the bias voltage in opposite direction, the current–voltage characteristic exhibited a hysteresis due to trapped charge. From the position of the point in the current–voltage characteristic at which a minimum current was attained (this point is called the neutrality point, see Fig. 2b), it was inferred that the fluorinated graphene and MLG films had p-type conductivity, with the sheet density of charge carriers as evaluated from the voltage shift ΔV g of neutrality point, p =7.2×1010 ΔV g, being (2–5)×1011 cm−2. The energy position of Fermi level in QDs as evaluated by the expression E F =ħν F(πp)0.5 [9] for several films treated in aqueous HF solution during times t ∼ t th, proved to be 0.05–0.08eV below the Dirac point.

The curve of the saturation current at positive bias voltages (see Fig. 2a) versus reciprocal temperature for a film functionalized to the state of conductor–insulator transition is shown in Fig. 7 . From the slopes of the curve, activation energies were determined, which proved to be E1=0.12±0.02eV and E2=0.35±0.02eV. The inset to the figure shows the assumed band diagram of the film with graphene QDs in fluorographene matrix. The electron work function Φ G in graphene and FLG films is known to be about 4.5–4.7eV [21,22] while the detailed band alignment between graphene and fluorographene is not presently known. Theoretical studies predict a high value of the electron work function Φ FG in fluorographene (about 6.0–7.3eV) [21,23], whereas experiments prove the existence of a barrier for the flow of charge carriers in the graphene/fluorographene/graphene structure [24]. The values of activation energies determined from the temperature dependence of electric current (Fig. 7) also confirm the existence of barriers in the system “graphene/fluorographene with embedded QDs”. The change of barrier height at temperature 230K is most likely due to the activation of charge carriers from different dimensional quantization levels in QDs to an energy level at which optimal tunneling of charge carriers across the barriers proceeds. Measurements of the temperature dependence of the current in initial and weakly fluorinated films (i.e. before the occurrence of the conductor–insulator transition) have showed that the current was either independent of temperature or showed a weakly decreasing behavior with temperature, thus exhibiting no activation slope on the curve of current versus temperature.

Processes allowing fabrication of arrays of graphene QDs of controllable sizes are of much interest since films with such QDs offer potential in many applications. Samples treated in HF solutions during a time sufficient for the occurrence of a conductor–insulator transition present fluorographene matrices with embedded graphene or MLG QD arrays. The sizes of graphene QDs in our samples proved to be less than 100nm, and the maximum density of QDs was 6×1010 cm−2. On increasing the treatment duration, the insulating regions grew in size while QD sizes showed a decrease; as a result, no further increase of QD density occurs. At QD sizes 10–20nm the density of QDs showed a reduction down to ∼1×1010 cm−2 due to more complete fluorination of fine QDs.

Restoration of electric conductivity in the films with multiple barriers was only possible in samples treated in aqueous solutions of hydrofluoric acid during times t ∼ t th. After longer treatments, QDs perfectly insulated from each other formed in our samples. The latter was confirmed in publication [25], where charge transient spectroscopy was employed to investigate the QD recharging process and typical relaxation times of charges trapped at QDs. In the study of [25], the possibility to charge QDs and, then, observe the emission of trapped charge carriers was also only found in graphene films treated in aqueous solutions of hydrofluoric acid during times of order the time of conductor–insulator transition.

As it was noted above, AFM lateral force measurements proved to be a most helpful technique providing rich visualization data for graphene QDs. Images taken from the surface of samples at different fluorination stages in this measurement mode had allowed us to examine the nucleation and evolution of QDs in the polycrystalline graphene and MLG films. We have to note here that, as the degree of sample fluorination increases, the contrast between the fluorinated portions of the film and QDs decays (Fig. 5e). The decay of the contrast is most likely related with the growth of fluorination degree of both the formed barriers and the film as a whole, including QDs. This assumption is confirmed by the Raman data that show that in the Raman spectra of samples treated in aqueous solution of HF for sufficiently long times (t ≫ t th) the amplitude of all peaks normally decreases below the noise level.

In [26,27], Natsuki et al. and Neek-Amal and Peeters have theoretically studied graphene buckling under a local change of lattice constant and generation of radial mechanical stresses. In [27], it was shown that graphene buckling may occur under rather moderate strains of 0.4%, with the critical size of buckled regions, defined as the minimal size for the occurrence of buckling, being about 6nm. According to the calculations of [26], an increase in the number of layers in an MLG film can be expected to have a pronounced influence on the formation of corrugations due to the interaction between layers induced by van der Waals forces. As a result, a change in corrugation period can be expected to occur in samples on increasing the film thickness. However, it was observed experimentally that the corrugation period remained roughly unchanged on increasing the film thickness from one monolayer to 8nm. This experimental fact calls for further study.

Recently, some studies of incomplete chemical functionalization which presumably leads to the formation of high-resistivity graphene derivatives such as hydrogeneted or oxidized graphene with formation of QD arrays were reported [6,28]. It should be noted here that, due to the high С – F bond energy, the QDs in fluorographene matrix obtained and examined in the present study feature higher stability and reproducibility of their properties; this makes such QDs highly promising objects for applications in comparison with less stable hydrogenated or oxidized graphene films.

To summarize, films involving arrays of graphene or MLG QDs in fluorographene matrix were obtained by treating polycrystalline graphene or MLG films with thickness up to several nanometers in a solution of hydrofluoric acid in water. It was shown that a proper choice of treatment duration, to be chosen approximately equal to the duration required for the transition of graphene or MLG films from conducting to insulating state to occur, permits creation of an array of conducting graphene islands, or graphene QDs, in the films, the QDs being isolated from each other by fluorographene potential barriers. AFM lateral force measurements allow easy visualization of formed graphene or MLG islands and determination of their sizes and density versus the functionalization degree of the films. The QD sizes that could be obtained using the developed functionalization procedure was estimated as ranging from 70–20nm (at QD density 6×1010 cm−2) to 10–20nm (at QD density 1×1010 cm−2). The conductivity type (р-type) and the concentration of charge carriers in QDs at room temperature, (2–5)×1011 cm−2, were determined.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Russian Foundation for Basic Research (Grant No. 12-02-01275) and by the Ministry of Science and Education of the Russian Federation. The authors are grateful to Dr. S.V. Golod for provided CVD-grown graphene samples and to Dr. V.A. Volodin for Raman measurements.
